Created: 2026-03-06T06:48:08.000Z
Metrics: {"retweet_count":15,"reply_count":3,"like_count":103,"quote_count":2,"bookmark_count":257,"impression_count":45943}
Article content: {"preview_text":"90% of vibe coded apps ship with security holes.
Not because the builders are bad. Because nobody told them the rules.
I've shipped 50+ MVPs across fintech, SaaS, and consumer apps for clients. And","title":"30 Security Rules to be the Top 1% VIBE CODER ","cover_media":"3_2029807252161658880","plain_text":"90% of vibe coded apps ship with security holes.
Not because the builders are bad. Because nobody told them the rules.
I've shipped 50+ MVPs across fintech, SaaS, and consumer apps for clients. And the number of production apps i've seen get breached, rate-limit abused, or flat-out wiped by a junior dev is insane.
So i wrote down every single rule i follow.
All 30 of them.
Authentication and Sessions
This is where most vibe coders mess up first. They let the AI build the auth.
Rule 1: Set session expiration
JWT tokens should max out at 7 days. Pair that with refresh token rotation. If you're not doing this, you're leaving every user session permanently exposed.
Rule 2: Never use AI-built auth
The AI will write you auth code. It will look clean. It will probably work.
It will also have edge cases that get you breached.
Use Clerk, Supabase Auth, or Auth0. These tools have entire security teams behind them. You don't.
Secrets and Environment
This one kills me everytime i see it. Devs pasting API keys into Cursor chats or pushing them to public repos.
Rule 3: Never paste API keys into AI chats
Use process.env. Always. No exceptions.
Rule 4: .gitignore is your first file
Not the last. Not an afterthought. Day one, first commit. Before you write a single line of application code.
Rule 5: Rotate secrets every 90 days
Set a calendar reminder. Treat it like rent. It's non-negotiable.
Packages and Dependencies
The AI suggests packages. Some of them don't exist. Some of them are outdated. Some are actively malicious.
Rule 6: Verify every package before installing
Search it on npm. Check the download count. Check the last publish date. If it looks off, it probably is.
Rule 7: Always ask for newer, more secure versions
The AI doesn't always default to latest. Ask it explicitly. "Use the most recent stable version."
Rule 8: Run npm audit fix right after building
Not before shipping. Right after building. Make it part of your workflow, not a checklist item you ignore.
Input and Data Security
Rule 9: Sanitize every input
Every single one. Forms, query params, file uploads. Use parameterized queries for anything hitting your database. SQL injection is old, but it still works.
Rule 10: Enable Row-Level Security from day one
If you're using Supabase, this is a toggle. Turn it on. Do not wait until you have users to think about data isolation.
Code Hygiene Before Shipping
Rule 11: Remove all console.log statements
You're leaking internal logic and sometimes sensitive data to anyone who opens devtools. Do a full sweep before every deploy.
Rule 12: CORS should only allow your production domain
Never use a wildcard. If you're seeing CORS errors in development, fix them properly. Don't just set the origin to *.
Rule 13: Validate all redirect URLs
Maintain an allow-list. Open redirects are one of the easiest attack vectors and one of the most overlooked.
API and Rate Limiting
Rule 14: Apply auth and rate limits to every endpoint
Including your mobile APIs. Not just the web ones. Mobile apps hit the same backend.
Rule 15: Rate limit everything from day one
100 requests per hour per IP is a starting point. Adjust based on your product. But start there.
Rule 16: Password reset routes get their own strict limit
3 attempts per email per hour max. Without this, you're open to email enumeration attacks and account lockout abuse.
Cost and Infrastructure
This one hits different when you're a solo founder and you wake up to a $10,000 AI API bill.
Rule 17: Cap AI API costs in your dashboard AND in your code
Both. Not one or the other. The dashboard cap saves you from runaway spend. The code-level cap saves you from a misconfigured dashboard.
Rule 18: Add DDoS protection
Cloudflare or Vercel edge config. Both are easy to set up, both have free tiers. There's no excuse to skip this.
Storage and Files
Rule 19: Lock down storage buckets
Users should only be able to access their own files. Not a shared folder. Not a public bucket. Their own files only.
Rule 20: Limit upload sizes and validate file types by signature
Not by extension. Someone can rename a malicious file to .jpg. Check the actual file signature. Most cloud storage providers have built-in tools for this.
Payments and Emails
Rule 21: Verify webhook signatures before processing any payment data
Stripe, Lemon Squeezy, all of them give you a webhook secret. Use it. Verify the signature on every incoming webhook. Otherwise anyone can POST fake payment events to your backend.
Rule 22: Use Resend or SendGrid with proper SPF/DKIM records
Your transactional emails will end up in spam without this. Set it up before you have users, not after.
Permissions and Logging
Rule 23: Check permissions server-side
UI-level checks are not security. They're UX. Anyone can call your API directly. Your server must enforce who can access what.
Rule 24: Ask the AI to act as a security engineer and review your code
Seriously, this is underused. Switch the prompt. Ask it to find vulnerabilities. It will flag things you completely missed.
Rule 25: Ask the AI to try and hack your app
It sounds weird. Do it anyway. It found an unprotected admin route in one of my MVPs that i had completley missed during development.
Rule 26: Log critical actions
Deletions, role changes, payments, data exports. If something goes wrong, you need a trail. Without logs, you're investigating blind.
Compliance and Environments
Rule 27: Build a real account deletion flow
GDPR is not optional if you have European users. And the fines are not small. Build the deletion flow before launch, not after your first legal inquiry.
Rule 28: Automate backups and test restoration
An untested backup is not a backup. It's a file. Schedule weekly restoration tests. At minimum, know how to restore from your last backup before you're in a crisis.
Rule 29: Keep test and production environments completely separate
Different database. Different API keys. Different everything. I've seen production data get corrupted by a test script one too many times.
Rule 30: Never let test webhooks touch real systems
Use Stripe test mode. Use sandbox environments. Never point your local dev webhook to a production endpoint.
The Real Talk
Ship fast.
But ship secure.
These 30 rules took me years of mistakes, client incidents, and middle-of-the-night panic to learn. Most of them take under 10 minutes to implement.
The ones that don't? They're the ones that save your entire product when something goes wrong.
Security isn't a feature you add after launch.
It's the foundation you build before the first user signs up.
Lock in and do it right."}
